title: One name for many values
checkLabels.t12: The total is not written by hand
checkLabels.t13: The count comes from the array
why.untouched: script.js is exactly as it was.
hint2.untouched: Try adding a sixth day. You will touch three different places.
why.not-linked: The script never reaches the page.
why.crashed: Your code stopped with an error.
hint2.crashed: Look at the Console panel below.
hint3.crashed: cups is not defined means the array does not exist under that name yet.
why.still-separate: The separate names are still there.
hint2.still-separate: The five constants disappear. One name is left.
why.no-array: There is no array called cups.
hint2.no-array: Name, equals, square brackets, and inside them the values separated by commas.
hint3.no-array: The order inside matters. It is exactly the order you write them in.
why.wrong-array: The values in the array are not right.
hint2.wrong-array: The five numbers are 4, 2, 6, 1 and 3, in that order.
hint3.wrong-array: Counting starts at zero. The first value sits at 0, the fifth at 4.
hint4.wrong-array: A value is taken out with the array's name and the number in square brackets.
hint3.wrong-total: The total is built up with a loop, exactly as in the section on repeating. The counter is the number of the value.
hint4.wrong-total: Start from zero and on every round add the value sitting at the current number.
why.hardcoded: The total is written by hand.
hint2.hardcoded: The number 16 should not appear in the file. It is arrived at, not written.
why.hardcoded-length: The number of days is written by hand.
hint2.hardcoded-length: The array knows how many values it carries. Do not tell it.
hint3.hardcoded-length: The array's name, a dot, length. The number changes on its own when you add a value.
why.wrong-count: There are not three lines in the console.
why.lost-p: A paragraph has disappeared from the page.
blocks.0.text: Five days, five cup counts. Five separate names that differ only by the digit at the end.
blocks.1.text: Tomorrow the shop opens on Saturday too. You add a sixth day and touch three places: a new name, a new term in the sum, and the five you wrote by hand.
blocks.3.text: An array is ordered values under one name. Each has an address — a number by which it is reached.
blocks.4.text: It is not a list that merely holds things side by side. The address is the point: any value can be taken out without knowing what surrounds it.
blocks.5.quote: It is written with square brackets. The order inside is exactly the one you wrote, and it does not change on its own.
blocks.7.text: The first value sits at zero. The second at one. It looks strange until you get used to it, and it causes half of all array mistakes.
blocks.9.text: sizes has three values. The first is at 0, the last at 2 — one less than the length. That is why the last one is taken with length minus one.
blocks.11.text: The length is not written by hand. The array knows it and will tell you. Add a value and the number changes on its own.
blocks.12.text: That is where the difference lies. Code that asks the array keeps working after the data changes. Code with a hand-written five quietly stays on yesterday's count.
blocks.14.text: Make an array called cups with the five numbers: 4, 2, 6, 1 and 3. The five separate names disappear.
blocks.15.text: The total is worked out with a loop, not written ready-made. Print the number of days, the total, and the last value — all three asked of the array.
